title: Ternary & Match 💎
description: One-line conditionals and Python's match statement.

slides.title: Ternary Operator ⚡
slides.content:
# One-Line if/else

Python has a shorthand called the **ternary operator** (or conditional expression) for simple if/else statements:

```
result = value_if_true if condition else value_if_false
```

**Example:**
```
age = 20
status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"
print(status)  # "adult"
```

It reads naturally: `status` is `"adult"` **if** age >= 18, **else** `"minor"`.

Use this for **simple, short** conditions only. For anything complex, stick to regular if/else for readability!

slides.title: Ternary Examples 🎬
slides.codeSnippet:
score = 75
result = "Pass" if score >= 70 else "Fail"
print(f"Score: {score} → {result}")

number = 7
parity = "even" if number % 2 == 0 else "odd"
print(f"{number} is {parity}")

temp = 28
feeling = "hot 🔥" if temp > 25 else "cool 🧊"
print(f"Temperature {temp}°C feels {feeling}")

quizData.question: What does x = 'yes' if True else 'no' assign to x?
quizData.explanation: The condition is True, so the value before 'if' is used: 'yes'.

slides.title: The match Statement 🎰
slides.content:
## Python's Switch (3.10+)

Python 3.10 introduced the **match** statement — similar to switch/case in other languages:

```
command = "start"

match command:
    case "start":
        print("Starting...")
    case "stop":
        print("Stopping...")
    case "pause":
        print("Pausing...")
    case _:
        print("Unknown command")
```

The `_` is the **wildcard pattern** — it matches anything (like `else`). The match statement is cleaner than long elif chains for checking a **single variable against many values**.

slides.title: match in Action 🎮
slides.content: Using match for a game command system:
slides.codeSnippet:
day = "Monday"

match day:
    case "Monday" | "Tuesday" | "Wednesday" | "Thursday" | "Friday":
        print(f"{day}: Work day 💼")
    case "Saturday" | "Sunday":
        print(f"{day}: Weekend! 🎉")
    case _:
        print("Not a valid day")

quizData.question: What does the _ pattern match in a match statement?
quizData.explanation: The underscore _ is a wildcard pattern that matches anything, acting as the default/fallback case.
